findings.title: Broken swap invariant allows draining nearly an entire reserve for dust-sized input

best_agent_match.title: Broken swap invariant allows draining about 99% of a reserve for dust input

findings.title: Pair token bindings remain mutable if the factory ever re-calls `initialize()`

best_agent_match.title: Pair can be reinitialized after deployment because `initialize()` is not one-time

rejected_candidates.title: Protocol fee formula over-mints LP shares to `feeTo`
rejected_candidates.reason: The code explicitly documents a `1/2th` sqrt(k) fee at line 88 and the implementation matches that comment. Without contrary protocol specs, this is an economic design choice rather than a demonstrated vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: Division by Zero in Price Oracle Calculation
rejected_candidates.reason: `_update()` only calls `uqdiv()` inside `if (timeElapsed > 0 && _reserve0 != 0 && _reserve1 != 0)`, so zero reserves do not trigger division by zero.

rejected_candidates.title: Unprotected skim Function Allows Anyone to Drain Excess Tokens
rejected_candidates.reason: `skim()` only transfers balances above the recorded reserves. This is standard Uniswap V2 behavior for recovering accidental excess tokens and does not let callers drain accounted LP reserves.

rejected_candidates.title: Unprotected sync Function Allows Reserve Manipulation
rejected_candidates.reason: `sync()` merely updates reserves to actual token balances. Any reserve change requires the caller to first move real tokens into the pair, so this is standard spot-price/oracle manipulation, not a standalone pair vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: IERC20 Interface Incompatibility with Non-Standard ERC20 Tokens
rejected_candidates.reason: The provided pair code only uses `balanceOf` and transfer-style calls; it never calls `name()`, `symbol()`, or `decimals()` on the underlying tokens. The optional metadata declarations in `IERC20` are inert here.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Zero Address Validation
rejected_candidates.reason: Only the factory can set `token0`/`token1`, so zero-address initialization is a trusted-factory misuse case. Zero-address approval/transfer behavior on the LP token does not create third-party theft or protocol-level breakage.

rejected_candidates.title: K Check Uses Integer Division Losing Precision
rejected_candidates.reason: The report misdiagnoses the issue. The real problem is the 10000-vs-1000 scaling mismatch in the invariant constant, not ordinary integer precision loss.

rejected_candidates.title: Permit Function Allows Exact Deadline Timestamp
rejected_candidates.reason: `deadline >= block.timestamp` is standard permit semantics and does not create meaningful exploitability.

rejected_candidates.title: Swap Callback Allows Arbitrary Data Execution
rejected_candidates.reason: The callback is the intended flash-swap hook. Reentrancy into pair state is blocked by `lock`, and no invariant bypass beyond the separate K-check bug is shown.

rejected_candidates.title: Fee On/Off Logic Can Leave Stale kLast Value
rejected_candidates.reason: Resetting `kLast` to zero when fees are off is standard Uniswap V2 behavior; re-enabling fees starts a new accounting baseline rather than creating an over-minting bug by itself.
